title: Spotify's AI promise: ‘We will not do deals that aren’t good for artists’ - Music Ally
text:
Spotify’s latest financial results
revealed a strong finish to 2025 for the company, as it added 38 million net new listeners in the fourth quarter alone, while reporting an operating profit of €701m in Q4.
The company ended the year with 751 million monthly active users (up 11% year-on-year) including 290 million premium subscribers (up 10%). Both figures were above Spotify’s previous guidance to analysts for the quarter.
Its Q4 revenues of €4.53bn were up 7% year-on-year, with 8% growth in subscriptions (to €4.01bn) outweighing a 4% decline in advertising revenues (to €518m).
For 2025 as a whole,
Spotify was a €17.19bn business
– $20.5bn at current exchange rates – while reporting an operating profit of just under €2.2bn.
Spotify’s earnings call
saw its new co-CEOs, Gustav Söderström and Alex Norström, take centre stage, and AI featured heavily both in their prepared remarks and in the questions they fielded from analysts.
“There is no question in my mind that we will continue to be one of the big beneficiaries of AI,” said Söderström, who described the goal for Spotify to become “the world’s most intelligent, agentic media platform, one that you can literally talk to, that fully understands each individual listener and puts them in the driver’s seat”.
After describing two categories of AI-music – making new songs and remixing existing tracks – he signified Spotify’s ambitions to launch features around the latter.
“Everything we see tells us listeners want to interact with their favourite music, and many artists want to let them, creating new revenue from their existing catalogue,” said Söderström.
“But in music, artists haven’t had a real way to monetize existing catalog through AI. Because the absence of a rights framework has kept AI mostly focused on the first category, net new creation. We want to work with the industry to fix that.”
This highlights a coming area of direct competition between Spotify and AI-music platforms Suno and Udio, whose licensing deals with labels have specifically pointed to ‘fans remixing artists’ as a key feature in their next-gen services.
A follow-up question put this to Spotify’s co-CEOs: of whether Suno, Udio, Klay Vision, Stability AI and others could “become DSPs that take share from Spotify” with their licensed services.
“This is a scaled service with a working business model. This is where you go to put your new songs, whichever technology or instrument or tool you use to create it,” said Norström.
“I’ve done the rounds, and no rightsholder is against our vision. We pretty much have the whole industry lined up behind us… We want to do it in a controlled way, respecting artists and the community, and we will not do deals that aren’t good for artists – and ultimately Spotify.”
Stuart Dredge
Music Ally's Head of Insight
More by Stuart Dredge
